assumptions: The question concerns consolidated financial statements under IFRS.
A foreign-currency intragroup dividend gives rise or may give rise to a recognised intragroup receivable/payable and related foreign exchange effects.

approaches.label: Fair value hedge
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: IFRS 9 identifies fair value hedge as a distinct hedge accounting model for recognised assets or liabilities exposed to a particular risk that could affect profit or loss.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: IFRS 9 identifies cash flow hedge as a distinct hedge accounting model for variability in cash flows of recognised items or highly probable forecast transactions.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: IFRS 9 and IFRIC 16 identify hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a separate hedge accounting model for foreign currency risk in consolidated financial statements.

approaches.label: Consolidation elimination accounting
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: IFRS 10 requires full elimination of intragroup assets, liabilities, income, expenses and cash flows in consolidated financial statements, making this a distinct accounting basis relevant to intragroup dividends and receivables.

approaches.label: Foreign currency accounting
approaches.rationale_for_inclusion: The context includes IFRS guidance that some intragroup foreign currency effects may remain relevant in consolidated financial statements, making ordinary foreign currency accounting a distinct non-hedge model within the issue.
